item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article took a different turn I wasn't expecting it to take. I don't know how I feel about this. I like the idea of aliens contacting us with strange stuff and possessing people. However, I have mixed feelings about how the story progresses.But I'm not a professional critic, so take this as you will XD.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want more Foundation contact with extraterrestrial/extradimensional races that aren't hell-bent on killing us. Even if it's one-way contact. I very much enjoyed the way this developed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! :O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The character development in such a short time is astounding. Very good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone of the final note from Clark is very off. It does not at all sound like a recording of spoken dialogue. It's way too proper and structured, and then there's a pause in there which is really jolting halfway through .

Edit: sorry, I forgot to say I liked it otherwise. I will upvote, but I hope you revise the final note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whattaya know, find-replacing a number does not replace its textual analogues. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also wanted to clear up the record on two-thousand-dash-N.

All we ever knew about two-thousand

Either way, two-thousand got attached to him

Could you see if maybe I could get hold of two-thousand-dash-N's old audio logs?

These should probably be changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
By god if I cannot have SCP-2999 I will have a damn good pop culture reference instead!

Major rewrite may happen.

item.title: Re: Open Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
I literally have no idea what got changed. I couldn't find the discrepancy on my personal copy.

Crisis averted, I am severely retarded.

item.title: Open Mod Post
item.content:encoded: This qualifies as a minor edit and is permissable under the rules. TL333s, you may decide if you wish to keep it or not. It looks legitimate to me, but I didn't stop to read the context thoroughly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know what you changed, but you probably shouldn't have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In document 2000-A the cylinder was designated SCP 2000-1, but once it was only SCP 2000 (wich was the alien in the old documentation)

As I consider this a minor edit I changed it.
If you consider it rude, sry =(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay. All I can really say to that is I intended this to be a "this shit just happened and we have no idea what's going on" scenario, coupled with "never trust the skips". When all you know about an anomaly comes from the anomaly itself, a certain amount of caution is in order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I tend to unleash my blather when excited by new ideas!

Some of that was just "thinking out loud" and trying to make my reasoning clear but the gist of it was a simple suggestion:

I feel like the Foundation would make clear to researchers both posible motivations of the aliens, and give enough information for the researchers to realize that either outcome could be terrible.

As it stands, I felt the SCP entry encourages the reader to judge the Foundation wrong for it's choice. That may be your intention, but the in-setting authors would want to avoid moral ambiguity1. Some researchers might be individually evil, and the Foundation may do evil things, but large scale actions seem to be accompanied by an insistance that it is for the greater good, even if the details are classified. I was just suggesting that at least the silhouette of the Foundations reasoning would be a welcome revision

Footnotes
1. The last thing the Foundation needs is personel trying to rescue 231, for example.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
yay someone's giving me feedback! ;_;

First, while I did understand the renumbering of the SCP, it felt unnecessary. I think the only reason to redesignate items in a SCP file would be to hide an object's absence, and I spent a while trying to figure out what that might be. You might want to keep the designations uniform or add a compelling reason (even if only hinted at) for it be redesignated.

The more I let this article sit, the more I think I'm going to need to change that eventually. The only things that make sense are blackboxing it or rewriting it in chronological order, so the cylinder is always 2000-1 even after the main skip's death.

As for the rest, I'm not sure if you're suggesting I change things or leave them as they are. Apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked your entry, but I do have a couple constructive criticisms if you revise it.

First, while I did understand the renumbering of the SCP, it felt unnecessary. I think the only reason to redesignate items in a SCP file would be to hide an object's absence, and I spent a while trying to figure out what that might be. You might want to keep the designations uniform or add a compelling reason (even if only hinted at) for it be redesignated.

Second, the Foundation isn't necessarily a monster for trying to stop an alien uplift program, and it doesn't seem like the kind of thing they would hide from authorized researchers. In our own history, contact between cultures has had all sorts of unforeseen consequences for both sides. I feel like the Foundation would make clear to researchers both posible motivations of the aliens, and give enough information for the researchers to realize that either outcome could be terrible.
If the weirdness of SCP is found universe-wide, then they definitely don't want the extra infinite layers of chaotic Keter-inviting complexity to visit Earth for any reason. If Earth is uniquely crazy, or even just an outlier of reality-taunting madness, it would be as much a service to the aliens as to Earth to keep our phenomena local. Right now, Earth could only propagate its infohazards and memetic contagions at the speed of radio waves (C at best). With FTL communications, we might be able to infect every succeptible sentient in our globular cluster in a very short time.
Another aspect of this point is that, right or wrong, the Foundation considers public ignorance to be vital. Given all the memetic, existential, metaphysical, and infohazard SCPs, I'm somewhat inclined to agree. Tossing that much hypertech into the world, even if the aliens had plans to avoid disrupting human society, might have unforeseen interactions with containment of one or more Keter-level threats, or just allow civilians to "know dangerous things"(literally).

In the world of SCP, the alien invasion might actually be preferable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now I understand.

I presented it this way for a couple of reasons. I wanted the interview to reveal that we had been duped all along (or had we?) and end on something of a zinger. Having the beginning in the middle takes you from a current state of (most of) what we know to a past state of what we thought we knew, so the answer of what's going on can be given in the final chapter.

I guess I just felt that "here's what's happening" was a better ending than "this is how things got like this", more of an EMB. I could consider restructuring it that way after the contest is over, leaving the final note from Dr. Clark at the end to keep the question of "Did Clark know?" hanging in the air. I don't know if it would work, but I feel like starting from the actual beginning would be a mistake, and I'm not sure how I'd format it, honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At both the start and the end, chronologically, the document is effectively the same. There's a being that receives transmissions from an object, and those transmissions are geared around conquest of the earth. The middle, again chronologically, is different. It shows that that might not really be what the aliens want. That's interesting.

But the order you present this in, EBM (end-beginning-middle), positions the similar ones next to each other. This feels redundant. The story arc is truncated, in that because of this order, the significance of the similarity of beginning and end is different.

I think telling the story in BME order could work for a more traditional style, with the Foundation deciding to cover up that the aliens may not be hostile could work. You could even trim off the E part, the final containment procedures after the researcher becomes the target of the transmissions. EM could work too, with the beginning largely implied. BEM could even work if you had heavy enough foreshadowing in the E part. But EBM order strikes me as among the weakest options.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That could work if the order were different, to highlight how the arc has gone back to where it started, but with one after the other, that's muted.

I've been staring at this for like ten minutes and I can't figure out what you mean. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somewhat torn on this. I don't like that the final article is pretty much the same as the first one, just with the designations swapped. That could work if the order were different, to highlight how the arc has gone back to where it started, but with one after the other, that's muted. So neutral for now, but there's a lot I do like here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a bit of Ender's Game in the "bug alien that's misunderstood" sense, but definitely different enough to ignore. One of my favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Appreciated. :) Good interviews are something I've stuggled with on this site. I need to write more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I insist that this article still contains too many generic elements.

But… the story it tells is honestly just too compelling for me to not upvote. Have yer +1, and well done.

EDIT: Also, side note, the interview is well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's actually a number I would much rather have for this. I have no illusions about winning. :B That said, you're the second person to mention that movie in the context of this article. Maybe I need to watch it again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gotta say, this somehow reminded me of the movie The Day the Earth Stood Still, with the cylinder and the hostile insect (which have reminded me of Kha'Zix from LoL). About the procedure 2000 Filigree and the problems with 2000's containment, am I at least partially right with the thought that they fill the gaps with some kind of a liquid sealing matter? Like clay, but of course more durable?

I also wondered about the bond 2000 had with Dr. Clark, which was elaborated upon in the last interview.

This is a very psychological, "character-driven", if you want, piece and provokes many thoughts. Whether 2000 was a liar or not, whether he is continuing his deception through 2000-1, or if he is actually right and his "people" want to help us… it asks many questions for the reader to answer. For me, <headcanon> 2000-1 was lying about the help as much as you can imagine </headcanon>

This is a first psychological take on 2000 I have seen here and for the execution, I award it with +1. Dunno if it should make 2000, it's certainly not bad, but very one-sided. So, no opinion on that. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one that's well written, but the concept just isn't grabbing me. I feel like I've seen the story before in many other forms, and for whatever reason it's just not affecting me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this comment for reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No doubt I pulled from numerous sources, but really it's just a big alien bug, no particular homage to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The file document is the original SCP-2000 article. It's completely separate, essentially the first draft of SCP-2000. The number was reassigned, it says so in the article, and I have no idea how to make it clearer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great. A near perfect pitch article to my tastes.
Good job, sir! O' so jouyous +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's covered in the text:
"General-access containment procedures are not to include this information, pursuant to Blackout Order 2000-145."

The reasoning is also straightforward. 2000-1 says he's not getting invasion transmissions. But he's an anomalous entity now. They don't trust him. Alternately, maybe they do believe him - and he was right that they would rather cover it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The initial description doesn't seem to mesh with what comes afterwards, particularly the interview. In the main description, it states that the transmissions are about an invasion, while the interview with 2000-1 directly contradicts that.

It works from a purely narrative progression, but it weakens my suspension of disbelief that 2000-1's statement doesn't seem to be recognized in the initial description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Question. It may just be the fact that most of the alien races I think of happen to also be the ones from Lovecraft, but was this creature inspired at all by the Mi-Go? That's not a bad thing, mind, because this works beautifully for my ideas of their society(well, mostly). Great all-around, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this one, great job

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its a little muddled up in regards to the SCP designation in the first description the object is referred to as SCP 2000 but in the file document its referred to as SCP 2000-1 and the creature is SCP 2000 that might make it a little confusing, also I don't know if the description of the object in the file document is needed as you have already described it in the first description above.

Just my thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This needs a Picard.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, that really is magic.

Thanks to ProcyonLotor and Nighkos for good feedback in the chats. This is my most ambitious article yet, I hope something comes of it.
